title: Why is Database Security important?
summary: Database security through encryption (SHA5, CREATE SCHEMA ENCRYPTION), access control (CREATE ROLE, GRANT, CREATE USER), and least privilege principles.
content_html:
Why is Database Security important?

Database contains sensitive information: customer information, employee salaries, medical records, financial data. Loss of database security can lead to:

  • Privacy violation (GDPR, Privacy Act)
  • Financial loss
  • Loss of company reputation

Three main layers of protection: Encryption (Encryption), Access control (Access Control), and SQL Injection Prevention.

1. Password Hashing

Never save passwords in plain text. Use a one-way hash function:

Create user table with password

CREATE TABLE user (
    id       INT NOT NULL,
    name     VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    password VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

INSERT with SHA2 hashing

INSERT INTO user VALUES (111, 'jane', SHA2('password', 256));

SHA2('password', 256) generates a hash of 64 hex characters. Reverse decoding is not possible. When logging in, hash the entered password and compare it with the saved hash.

MySQL also supports:

  • SHA1() — 40 hex characters (no longer safe)
  • SHA2(str, 256) — SHA-256 (recommended)
  • SHA2(str, 512) — SHA-512 (stronger)
  • MD5() — 32 hex characters (unsafe, do not use)

2. Database-level Encryption

MySQL supports encryption of the entire schema (all tables in that schema):

CREATE SCHEMA db1 DEFAULT ENCRYPTION='y';

CREATE TABLE db1.user (
    id       INT,
    name     VARCHAR(100),
    password VARCHAR(50)
);

With ENCRYPTION='y', data on disk is automatically encrypted. Even if someone gets the physical file of the database, they cannot read the data.

You can set encryption for each table:

CREATE TABLE SensitiveData (
    id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    data VARCHAR(200)
) ENCRYPTION='y';

3. Access Control — Access control by Role

Principle Least Privilege: each user only has the necessary rights, no more.

Step 1: Create Role

CREATE ROLE accountant;
CREATE ROLE manager;
CREATE ROLE readonly_user;

Step 2: Assign permissions to Role

GRANT SELECT ON employee TO accountant;
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON salary TO accountant;

GRANT SELECT ON employee TO readonly_user;

GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON company_db.* TO manager;

Permissions that can be GRANT:

PrivilegeDescription
SELECTRead data
INSERTAdd new line
UPDATEEdit existing data
DELETEDelete line
CREATECreate table/database
DROPDelete table/database
ALL PRIVILEGESAll rights reserved

Step 3: Create User

CREATE USER jane IDENTIFIED BY 'initialpassword123';
CREATE USER bob IDENTIFIED BY 'securePass456!';

Step 4: Assign Role to User

GRANT accountant TO jane;
GRANT readonly_user TO bob;
GRANT manager TO alice;

REVOKE — Revoke permissions

REVOKE DELETE ON salary FROM accountant;
REVOKE accountant FROM jane;

4. View Permissions — Protect sensitive data

Use View to hide sensitive columns:

CREATE VIEW EmployeePublicInfo AS
SELECT EmployeeID, Name, Department, JobTitle
FROM Employee;

GRANT SELECT ON EmployeePublicInfo TO readonly_user;

User readonly_user sees EmployeePublicInfo but does not see the original Employee table (with columns Salary, SSN, ...).

5. Check permissions

SHOW GRANTS FOR jane;

SHOW GRANTS FOR CURRENT_USER;
